coaching.coaching: Pin down exact scope of the S4 neutrality gate with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: exact scope of the S4 neutrality gate

coaching.coaching: Plan how noqa re-export coverage in the neutrality gate will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: noqa re-export coverage in the neutrality gate

coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] acceptance-criteria completeness and precision will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: acceptance-criteria completeness and precision

coaching.coaching: Pin down opt-in capability Protocol detection rule with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: opt-in capability Protocol detection rule

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for registry-derived vs. static-frozenset config validation.
coaching.subject: registry-derived vs. static-frozenset config validation

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for stale ADR section update ownership across stories to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: stale ADR section update ownership across stories

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the site count in S4: if '8 vendor-importing modules + 4 transport/construction sites = 12' is correct, update the AC bullet to '12 sites'; if some overlap reduces it to 10, update the S4 decomposition narrative to show the deduction explicitly.
evidence: S4 decomposition: 'the 8 confirmed vendor-importing core modules **and** the 4 transport/construction sites (applier/fetcher/run_differs/_attestation)'
AC S4 bullet: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites'
findings.finding: The S4 decomposition body counts '8 confirmed vendor-importing core modules AND 4 transport/construction sites' (implying 12 total sites to neutralize), but the S4 AC bullet says 'neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites' — a numeric contradiction between sections that will send the implementer in two directions when writing the gate.
findings.location: Child decomposition S4 vs. Acceptance Criteria S4 bullet ('neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites')
scenarios: Implementer writes a neutrality gate covering 10 sites but misses 2 (or vice-versa, writes one for 12 and the AC check fails at 10).
Reviewer cannot confirm the gate is complete without knowing the authoritative count.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the AC S4 bullet explaining the arithmetic: e.g., '…the 12 sites (8 vendor-importing modules + 4 transport/construction sites)' — or if 10 is correct, add '(8 core modules + 2 of the 4 construction sites; applier and fetcher are already indirect)' to the decomposition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the neutrality gate site count: update parent AC3 from '10' to '12' (or enumerate the exact 12 sites) and confirm the gate in S4 covers all 12.
evidence: Parent AC3: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites'
S4 Scope: '8 core importer sites + 4 transport/construction sites' = 12
S4 AC1: 'None of the 12 rewired core modules import adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules'
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC3 states the neutrality gate covers '10 differ/apply sites,' but S4 confirms 12 rewired sites (8 vendor-importing core modules + 4 transport/construction sites). If the gate is implemented against the parent's stale count of 10, two sites risk being excluded from the gate, leaving them unchecked.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 — Acceptance Criteria §1 (neutrality grep gate); parent AC3
scenarios: A gate implemented from the parent spec's list of 10 sites omits apply_inbound_records.py (the 'jira' literal site) and one other, leaving those sites unreachable by the neutrality check and undetected if regressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent AC3 to read 'neutrality gate on the 12 rewired sites' and list them explicitly; ensure S4's grep/test gate enumerates all 12 module names.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S4 scope: enumerate the `outbound_differ.py` `# noqa: F401` re-export surface (`_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, `_map_local_to_jira_fields`) and explicitly include the tests that access these names via `outbound_differ.<name>` (e.g. `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85`) in the atomic rewire set, so the neutrality gate and the test suite can both pass.
evidence: `outbound_differ.py:63-69`: `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import (  # noqa: F401\n    _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, _assignee_matches, _diff_fields, _extract_jira_field, _map_local_to_jira_fields,\n)`
`test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85`: `assert "session_log" not in outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`

S4 scope counts '8 confirmed vendor-importing core modules' and '23 test files use that seam' for `_load_acli` — but does not enumerate the secondary test surface that accesses vendor names through `outbound_differ.<name>` re-exports

S4 neutrality gate AC: 'None of the 12 rewired core modules import adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules' — removing the `outbound_fields` import from `outbound_differ.py` drops these re-exported names unless the tests are also rewritten
findings.finding: S4's neutrality gate and rewiring scope do not account for the `# noqa: F401` re-exports in `outbound_differ.py` (lines 63-77), which expose vendor names (`_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, `_map_local_to_jira_fields`) as `outbound_differ.<name>` for tests. At least `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` asserts `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, and the pattern is used implicitly wherever tests load `outbound_differ` and call vendor-originated helpers on it. Removing the direct vendor imports (to satisfy the neutrality gate) without also updating these re-export-dependent tests will cause breakage that neither the 'rewired sites' list nor the 'patched tests' count covers.
findings.location: S4 story (`6673-7636-a116-4f90`) — 'Scope' section listing 8 core importer sites; AC neutrality gate; `outbound_differ.py:63-77`; `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85`
scenarios: S4 implementer removes `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE ...` from `outbound_differ.py` to satisfy the neutrality gate; `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py` fails with `AttributeError: module 'outbound_differ' has no attribute '_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE'`

Implementer keeps the re-exports to avoid breaking tests, but then the neutrality gate grep flags `outbound_differ.py` as still importing from a root vendor module — the story's own AC is unsatisfiable without also migrating these test assertions
findings.suggested_fix: In S4's scope, add: 'Rewrite tests that access `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` (and any other re-exported vendor names) to import those symbols directly from `adapters/jira/` (or via the Backend mapper) once the re-exports are removed.' Add a grep step in S4 planning: `grep -r 'outbound_differ\._LOCAL_TO_JIRA\|outbound_differ\._assignee\|outbound_differ\._diff_fields\|outbound_differ\._extract_jira\|outbound_differ\._map_local' tests/` to enumerate the full surface before implementation.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In S2/ADR §(d), specify the positive capability-detection rule (e.g., `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)`, a `backend.capabilities: frozenset[str]` sentinel, or `hasattr`) so it is co-located with the Backend Protocol definition and the contract suite can verify it.
evidence: S2 ticket Testing section: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'
The plan rules out structural introspection but provides no positive rule for the detection mechanism that must be co-located with the Backend Protocol definition in _backend.py
S4 rewires all core consumers through the Backend port — the core must invoke the capability check somewhere, but no call site or decision rule is named
Grep confirms SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental, IdentityConvention do not yet exist in codebase — plan is the sole spec
findings.finding: The opt-in capability Protocol detection rule is referenced but never specified: S2 says capability methods are exercised 'only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior … not via structural introspection)' but never defines the positive protocol — how the core or contract suite determines whether a backend advertises SupportsLinks/SupportsComments/SupportsIncremental (isinstance check? sentinel attribute? try/except AttributeError?).
findings.location: S2 (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) Testing section: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'
scenarios: S4 implementer routes outbound_links.py through the Backend port but must decide: does every Backend get asked for link sync, or only those that isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)? The plan's 'not via structural introspection' prohibition rules out isinstance but provides no alternative.
FakeBackend in the contract suite must decide whether to advertise SupportsLinks — with no detection rule specified, the contract suite cannot verify that a non-advertising backend is skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to ADR §(d) and S2's _backend.py spec: 'Capability detection uses isinstance(backend, Supports<X>); the Backend facade exposes no separate capability flag. The contract suite parameterizes over a FakeBackend that does and does not implement SupportsLinks to verify the core skips link sync for the non-implementing variant.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S3 whether `config.reconciler.backend` validation derives its allowed keys directly from the in-tree registry (so registering a new backend is sufficient) or requires a separate static-list update — and if the latter, document it as a deliberate two-step requirement.
evidence: 'add `reconciler.backend` config (static-key validated) + an in-tree registry + `select_backend()`'
'adding a backend requires only a new `adapters/<x>/` implementing the pinned interface' (AC last bullet) — this would be violated if config validation also needs updating
ABSENCE: the plan never states that config validation is derived from registry membership
findings.finding: The plan describes `config.reconciler.backend` as 'static-key validated' in S3 but does not clarify whether the config validator derives its allowed set of backend keys from the in-tree registry (open/extensible) or from a separate hardcoded list (closed). If it is a separate static list, adding a new backend (epic be74) requires editing both the registry AND the config validator — a silent coupling that undermines the stated extensibility goal.
findings.location: S3 `94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75` description; AC bullet 2 ('backend is chosen by `config.reconciler.backend` via the registry')
scenarios: Epic be74 implements a GitHub Issues backend, registers it in the registry, but the config validator still only accepts 'jira' — causing a confusing config-load error at runtime instead of a seamless plug-in experience.
A future contributor follows the 'implement adapters/<x>/' guide, misses the config validator update, and ships a backend that silently falls back to jira.
findings.suggested_fix: Have `select_backend()` / the registry expose its registered keys, and point the config validator's `Literal` or `Enum` at that set — or explicitly document that adding a backend requires both a registry entry and a config-validator update, so the be74 epic spec includes that step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In S3, specify the decision rule for keeping `_config_schema.py`'s static frozenset in sync with the registry (e.g., derive the frozenset from the registry at schema-load time via a lazy import, or add a test that asserts `static_keys == set(registry.keys())` after all adapters are imported).
evidence: S3 Approach: 'a static frozenset of known backend keys (e.g. {"jira"}). The schema is stdlib-only / zero-rebar-dep (it cannot import the registry)'
The plan names epic be74 as the first real second backend — adding it requires updating the static frozenset AND registering in the registry, but no co-location rule or sync test is specified
S3 Testing section does not include a test that the static frozenset and registry are in sync (only that a config with 'nope' is rejected at config-load)
findings.finding: The reconciliation rule between the static frozenset in `_config_schema.py` and the runtime registry is never specified: S3 introduces a static `frozenset({'jira'})` for config-load validation (because the schema 'cannot import the registry') alongside the runtime registry, but never states who owns updates when a new backend is added in-tree, nor whether a mismatch between the two is detectable/enforced.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) Approach: 'validate the key against a static frozenset of known backend keys (e.g. {"jira"}). The schema is stdlib-only / zero-rebar-dep (it cannot import the registry), so the static set guards config-load; the registry does the authoritative runtime lookup'
scenarios: A second-backend author adds adapters/github/ and registers 'github' in the registry but forgets to update the static frozenset — config-load silently rejects 'github' even though the backend is installed.
Conversely, the frozenset is updated to include 'github' before the adapter is registered, causing select_backend() to fail at runtime with a KeyError.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sync-check test in S3: after importing all adapters (triggering registration), assert that the static frozenset equals `frozenset(registry.keys())`. This makes the mismatch a CI failure rather than a silent production bug.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand S1's scope to also correct the stale §(a) module inventory (remove baseline_shadow.py, add apply_inbound.py) so the ADR is internally consistent after the epic lands.
evidence: S4 Context: 'the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names baseline_shadow.py, which does not exist, and omits apply_inbound.py'
S1 Scope: 'Revised §(d): … Status/Decision/Consequences edits for consistency. No code changes.' — §(a) not mentioned
No other sibling names §(a) correction in its scope
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S1 (ef33) revises ADR 0035 §(d) only; it does not update §(a), which S4 explicitly identifies as stale (§(a) names non-existent `baseline_shadow.py` and omits `apply_inbound.py`). After all stories land, the ADR will contain a stale and incorrect module inventory in §(a), contradicting the post-epic codebase state.
findings.location: child ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb — Scope; child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 — Context/Problem (ADR §(a) stale note); residual-references mode
scenarios: A future contributor adding a third backend consults ADR 0035 §(a) for the list of coupling sites, finds baseline_shadow.py (doesn't exist) and misses apply_inbound.py, leading to an incomplete integration.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S1's edit-set: 'Update ADR §(a) coupling inventory: remove baseline_shadow.py (does not exist), add apply_inbound.py:49 as a confirmed leak site.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'repoint the two root modules that import by sibling path (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) to their new adapters/jira/ locations' explicitly to the S5 decomposition bullet, and clarify the mechanism (import-path update, no shim).
evidence: S5 decomposition: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/, teach the file-location loader the new dir, and migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims)' — no mention of inbound_fields.py or outbound_comments.py
AC S5 bullet: 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed'
findings.finding: The S5 AC bullet names a specific deliverable — 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed' — that does not appear in the S5 decomposition body, leaving that deliverable without a corresponding description of what 'repointing' entails.
findings.location: Child decomposition S5 vs. Acceptance Criteria S5 bullet
scenarios: Implementer completes S5 per the decomposition description but misses repointing those two files, breaking imports at runtime while the decomposition text gave no signal this was required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC clause for S3 that verifies unknown backend keys are rejected at config-load time (e.g., 'an unrecognised backend key raises a clear ConfigError at startup').
evidence: S3 decomposition: 'add reconciler.backend config (static-key validated)'
AC S3 bullet: 'The backend is chosen by config.reconciler.backend via the registry; the default ("jira") reproduces today's behavior end-to-end' — no validation/rejection check
findings.finding: The S3 decomposition specifies 'static-key validated' for config.reconciler.backend, but no AC bullet verifies that the config validation rejects unknown backend keys — leaving a specified behavior with no acceptance check.
findings.location: Child decomposition S3 vs. Acceptance Criteria S3 bullet
scenarios: A typo in a config file (e.g. 'jjira') silently falls through if validation is never exercised, and the implementer may skip it believing the AC is already met by the happy-path check.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the S3 AC bullet: '…; an unrecognised value for config.reconciler.backend raises a ConfigError (static-key validation).'}

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out/rollback plan for S5 — e.g., describe whether S5 must land as a single atomic commit (squash-merge), what the revert commit looks like, and confirm that temporary shims are acceptable only as a last-resort revert mechanism.
evidence: 'migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims)'
'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/'
No section anywhere in the plan describes a rollback or back-out procedure if S5 lands partially or CI fails post-merge.
findings.finding: The plan has no stated rollback or back-out path for the S5 physical module relocation ('no re-export shims'). If the S5 landing fails mid-way (e.g., CI red after some patch-string migrations are merged), there is no described mechanism to revert without introducing the shims the plan explicitly forbids.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S5 and AC bullet 4 ('no re-export shims')
scenarios: S5 is merged in parts (e.g., file moves merged, then test-patch migration fails CI); team must either add temporary shims (violating the stated constraint) or manually revert a large commit under time pressure.
A future bisect of a regression lands on the S5 boundary with no clean revert strategy documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note under S5: 'S5 must land as a single squash-merge commit. If CI is red post-merge, the back-out is a revert of that single commit; no intermediate shims are needed because the revert restores the original paths atomically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name where `reconciler.backend` (valid values, default, effect) will be documented — e.g., add an entry to the config reference doc or a dedicated subsection in ADR 0035 §(d).
evidence: 'add reconciler.backend config (static-key validated) + an in-tree registry + select_backend() + JiraBackend registration'

ADR 0035 §(d) is tasked to pin the Backend *interface* (Protocols, RemoteRef), but no plan section states that the config key itself, its valid values, and its default are captured in user-facing documentation.
Absence: no mention of config-reference doc, README update, or operator guide for the new key.
findings.finding: The new `reconciler.backend` config key introduced in S3 is a user-facing configuration entry, but the plan does not state where it will be documented (config reference, README, operator guide, or ADR) beyond being 'static-key validated' in code.
scenarios: An operator upgrading rebar sees an unknown config key in error messages or release notes and has no doc to consult.
A contributor implementing the first real second backend (epic be74) cannot find the canonical list of valid backend keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale in S3 (or ADR 0035 §(d)) explaining why the bespoke `@register` decorator is preferred over `pluggy` (which is already in the lockfile) — e.g. `pluggy` uses caller-side hook specs rather than implementor-side factory registration, making it a semantic mismatch for a single-active-backend selection model.
evidence: `.github/release-requirements.txt:192: pluggy==1.6.0` — pluggy is already in the project's pinned dependency graph.
S3 description states 'no setuptools entry-points; a second backend is added in-tree under adapters/<x>/' with no mention of pluggy.
The plan hand-rolls `_reset_registry_for_test()` for test isolation — precisely the kind of concern pluggy's hook-call system handles via its own call context isolation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke in-tree decorator registry (`register` / `select_backend` / `_reset_registry_for_test`) without acknowledging that `pluggy` — a purpose-built plugin/hook registry — is already present in the project's lockfile dependency graph.
findings.location: S3 (`94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75`) description — 'an in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere — no setuptools entry-points'
scenarios: A reviewer or future implementer evaluating the registry design has no documentation that `pluggy` was considered and found unsuitable.
If the registry grows (more backends, per-hook call ordering), the bespoke implementation will need to re-solve problems pluggy already solves.
findings.suggested_fix: In the S3 description or ADR 0035 §(d), add: '`pluggy` (present in the lockfile as a transitive) was considered but not used: its caller-owns-the-hookspec model is designed for multi-hook fan-out, not single-active-backend selection; the simpler decorator-registry style already used by `@register_step` in `workflow/executor.py` is the correct idiom here.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In ADR §(d) and S2's _backend.py spec, define the value vocabulary for `RemoteRef`: what `vendor` values are canonical (and whether they must match registry keys), what `instance` represents and its format, and what `remote_id` format is expected.
evidence: Plan Design section: 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal in apply_inbound_records.py and the bare jira_key'
S2 AC only verifies that the constructor call succeeds — no value-vocabulary constraint stated
S3 registry key is 'jira' (a string); S4 replaces hardcoded 'jira' with RemoteRef — but the mapping from RemoteRef.vendor to registry key is never pinned
apply_inbound_records.py:70,74 passes 'jira' as vendor to rebar.ensure_identity_for() — the replacement RemoteRef must carry equivalent semantics but the contract is unspecified
findings.finding: The `RemoteRef.vendor` value vocabulary is never defined: S2 introduces `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` to replace hardcoded `'jira'` literals, and S3 keys the registry on `config.reconciler.backend` string (e.g. `'jira'`), but the plan never states whether `RemoteRef.vendor` must equal the registry key, what format `instance` takes (URL? hostname? arbitrary string?), or what constraints apply to `remote_id`.
findings.location: Design section: 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal'; S2 AC: 'RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…) constructs'; S4: 'replace apply_inbound_records.py "jira" literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'
scenarios: S4 implementer constructing RemoteRef must choose a value for vendor — is it 'jira' (matching the registry key), 'atlassian', or something else? The ADR §(d) sketch does not say.

A second-backend author implementing adapters/github/ must know what vendor string to put in their RemoteRef to make IdentityConvention and rebar.ensure_identity_for() work correctly — the value vocabulary is the interface contract they need.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 800-LOC module-size AC to S4, mirroring S5's AC4, to enforce the cap at every landed change as the parent requires.
evidence: Parent AC5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) throughout'
S5 AC4: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap after the move — verified by the module-size gate'
S4 has no corresponding AC for the LOC cap
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC5 requires the 800-LOC module-size cap to hold 'throughout' (i.e., at every landed change), but S4 — the story with the largest core-module edits (routing 12 sites, adding Backend calls, relocating inlined logic) — does not include an explicit LOC-cap verification AC. The cap is only gated in S5.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 — Acceptance Criteria (no LOC cap AC present); parent AC5
scenarios: S4 routes 12 core sites through Backend calls and adds RemoteRef/IdentityConvention usage; if a core module grows past 800 LOC during S4, it violates the parent AC at the moment S4 lands, and the violation persists undetected until S5's gate runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S4's Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py remains within the 800-LOC cap after S4 lands — verified by the module-size gate (.github/module-size-limit.txt).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `adapters/jira/backend.py` to S5's surviving-reference repoint list and update its AcliClient import to the new intra-adapter path after acli.py moves.
evidence: S5 Scope lists: 'jira_fields.py:18', 'inbound_fields.py:36', 'acli_cli_ops.py:36', 'any S4-residual loader refs' — backend.py is absent
S2 creates adapters/jira/backend.py delegating to acli.AcliClient
S5 moves acli.py to adapters/jira/ — making the old rebar_reconciler.acli dotted import invalid
S4 notes 'acli.py:145 (adapter-internal client construction) stays in the adapter, moves in S5'
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S5's explicit surviving-reference edit-set omits `adapters/jira/backend.py` (created in S2, modified in S3/S4), which imports `AcliClient` from the root `rebar_reconciler.acli` path. After S5 physically moves `acli.py` to `adapters/jira/acli.py`, this import breaks — but `backend.py` is not listed in S5's files to repoint.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 — Scope / Edit-set (surviving references list); consumer impact mode
scenarios: After S5's atomic move, `from rebar_reconciler.acli import AcliClient` inside `adapters/jira/backend.py` becomes a broken import, causing an ImportError at runtime and failing the full test suite — despite the structural gate passing because backend.py is not in the grep pattern for 'old root paths'.
findings.suggested_fix: In S5's edit-set, add: 'adapters/jira/backend.py — `from rebar_reconciler.acli import AcliClient` → `from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.acli import AcliClient`' and include it in the zero-hits grep gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S4 should enumerate the specific adapters/jira/ destination files for each relocated logic piece (ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, value-maps, link constants), and S5 should explicitly verify no new files created by S4 contain references to moved root-level modules.
evidence: S4 Scope: 'Relocate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/ (single-sourced)'
S4 Approach: does not name specific destination files within adapters/jira/
S5 Edit-set: names only the eight root-level modules to move plus three specific reference files — no mention of any new S4-created helper files under adapters/jira/
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S4 says it 'relocates ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent mapping, duplicated value-maps, and outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/' but does not specify which file(s) within adapters/jira/ receive each piece of relocated logic. If S4 creates new helper files under adapters/jira/ (beyond the already-known backend.py), those files are not enumerated in S5's edit-set — S5 may not know to include them in its grep gate or structural checks.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 — Scope / Approach (destination of relocated logic unspecified); child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 — Scope (scope-gap corollary)
scenarios: S4 adds a new adapters/jira/jira_link_constants.py to single-source the link-constant map. S5's grep gate checks only the eight named moved files; jira_link_constants.py is not gated, and if it somehow imports from the old root outbound_links path (or is not co-located correctly), it escapes both S4's neutrality gate and S5's structural gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S4's Approach: a table mapping each relocated logic piece to its destination file under adapters/jira/ (e.g., backend.py methods vs. new helper modules). Reference these destination files explicitly in S5's grep gate scope.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
